coredata.dc:title: Efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of dopamine using a dual-emission carbon dot-gold nanocluster nanohybrid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanyuan
Xu, Hedan
Yang, Yuling
Zhu, Fengmei
Pu, Yunxun
You, Xiaoshuang
Liao, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent probe is prepared based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for fluorometric determination of dopamine (DA). The ratiometric fluorescent probe (CQDs-AuNCs) was obtained by coupling amidated modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with bovine serum albumin-coated gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which had two emission peaks of 446 nm and 670 nm and displayed purple fluorescence under the excitation of 397 nm. The probe is based on the performance that the purple fluorescence of the CQDs-AuNCs is quenched by DA due to the electron transfer from CQDs to DA and thus inhibiting the FRET process between CQDs and AuNCs. The ratiometric fluorescent probe is selective for DA with a detection limit of 2.66 nM. Response is linear in the ranges of 2.66 nM–0.18 mM, 0.511–3.79 mM and 4.87–13.1 mM. The method has good anti-interference ability even if the concentration of the interfering substance is 5 times higher than that of DA. Due to the high sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, this method was successfully used to measure DA in serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Fluorescent probe
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoclusters
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity test of CQDs-AuNCs to DA
Assays of DA detection in real samples
Possible mechanism of the sensing DA
Selectivity and sensitivity test of the DA probe
Ratiometric fluorescent probe detection of DA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of blue-emitting CQDs (A), red-emitting AuNCs (B), and dual-emitting CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrids (C). Inset: the photographs of the corresponding products under daylight (left) and excitation with a UV lamp at 365 nm (right). (D) Time course curves of the two fluorescence emission peaks of CQDs-AuNCs.

(A) Emission spectrum of the CQDs (blue line) and excitation spectrum of AuNCs (black line). (B) TEM images of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid. (Inset is the high-resolution TEM image). (C) Particle size distribution histogram and Gauss fitting of CQDs-AuNCs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of AuNCs, CQDs and CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid.
Fluorescent spectra of CQDs, AuNCs and CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs (A), AuNCs (B) and CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (C) before (red line) and after (blue line) adding 5 mM DA. (D) The photographs of CQDs (a, a’), AuNCs (c, c’) and CQDs-AuNCs (b, b’) before (a, b, c) and after (a’, b’, c’) adding 5 mM DA under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-AuNCs (λex = 390 nm) in the absence and presence of different concentration DA; Inset: photograph obtained upon excitation with a UV lamp at 365 nm. (B) Linear plotting relationship between F446/F670 and DA concentration. Inset is the linear region of DA concentration from 2.66 nM to 13.1 mM.
The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe for DA detection. The concentration of DA is 0.2 mM, and the concentrations of all the interferents are 1 mM.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescent probe CQDs-AuNCs for the detection of DA.
A brief summary of different nanomaterials-based fluorescence probes for the detection of DA.

row.entry: Ratiometric fluorescent method (GSH-DHLA-Ag NCs and His-Ag NCs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of DA in spiked human serum samples (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of dopamine using a dual-emission carbon dot-gold nanocluster nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Engineering Laboratory of Nano/Micro Biomedical Detection, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Nano/Micro Composite Materials and Devices, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, No. 12 East Road, University Town, Chongqing, 401331, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Engineering Laboratory of Nano/Micro Biomedical Detection
Chongqing Key Laboratory of Nano/Micro Composite Materials and Devices
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Engineering Laboratory of Nano/Micro Biomedical Detection, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Nano/Micro Composite Materials and Devices, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, No. 12 East road, University town, Chongqing 401331, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of a ratiometric fluorescent probe CQDs-AuNCs used for fluorometric determination of dopamine (DA) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

A ratiometric fluorescent probe is prepared based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for fluorometric determination of dopamine (DA). The ratiometric fluorescent probe (CQDs-AuNCs) was obtained by coupling amidated modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with bovine serum albumin-coated gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which had two emission peaks of 446 nm and 670 nm and displayed purple fluorescence under the excitation of 397 nm. The probe is based on the performance that the purple fluorescence of the CQDs-AuNCs is quenched by DA due to the electron transfer from CQDs to DA and thus inhibiting the FRET process between CQDs and AuNCs. The ratiometric fluorescent probe is selective for DA with a detection limit of 2.66 nM. Response is linear in the ranges of 2.66 nM–0.18 mM, 0.511–3.79 mM and 4.87–13.1 mM. The method has good anti-interference ability even if the concentration of the interfering substance is 5 times higher than that of DA. Due to the high sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, this method was successfully used to measure DA in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a crucial catecholamine neurotransmitter and hormone, which plays an important role in many behavioral responses and brain activities such as sensation, addiction, emotion and exercise [1,2]. The concentration of DA in biological fluids (such as plasma, urine, and extracellular fluids in the central nervous system) affects human health, and abnormal DA concentrations may be an important indicator of many diseases [3–6]. For example, excessive secretion of DA (as caused by Huntington's disease) is associated with energy metabolism disorders and can lead to sudden death [4]. The lack of DA can cause muscles to lose control, leading to distractions and even Parkinson's disease [5]. In addition, schizophrenia and anorexia have also been confirmed to be associated with abnormal DA concentrations [6]. Therefore, achieving sensitive detection of DA has a very positive effect on human health detection and prevention and treatment of various diseases. However, the concentration of DA in biological systems is very low (about 0.1 mM–1.0 mM) [7]. How to achieve qualitative and quantitative detection of low concentration DA is a challenge for researchers.

A number of techniques for detecting DA have been developed, including high performance liquid chromatography [8], mass spectrometry [9], colorimetric analysis [10], and electrochemical techniques [11,12]. These technologies are widely used in the biochemical field because of their advantages of accurate detection and high precision. However, these technologies also have shortcomings. For example, chromatography and mass spectrometry require expensive instrument and cumbersome sample preparation procedures; colorimetric analysis is relatively less sensitive; electrochemical methods are often interfered with substances with similar oxidation potentials, such as ascorbic acid and uric acid. Compared with the above detection method, the fluorescence sensing method has the advantages of low cost, simplicity, strong practicability and excellent selectivity, and is an alternative method with good potential [13–16]. In addition, it does not require DA separation from the sample to achieve trace detection [17].

At present, the probes commonly used in fluorescence sensing analysis include organic substances [18], carbon dots [19], semiconductor quantum dots [20], and metal nanoclusters [13,14,21–23]. For example, the carbon dots have been used for selective and sensitive sensing of metal ions [19], small molecules [24], and biopolymers [25]. Metal nanoclusters, such as gold nanoclusters, silver nanoclusters and so on, as fluorescent probes have drawn more and more attention due to their fluorescent properties, big Stocks shift and good biocompatibility [26], and are widely used in metal ion detection, small biological molecule detection, protein detection and biological metabolism analysis [10,13,14,27]. However, when they are used as single-wavelength fluorescent sensing probes, the fluorescence intensity is easily affected by factors such as the drift of the detector’s photoelectric system, the probe concentration, and auto fluorescence in complex biological systems, making them susceptible to interference in quantitative detection. Ratiometric fluorescent method can overcome these disadvantages as it takes the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at two different wavelengths as the response signal to determine the concentration of the target [7,28–30]. Thus it is not affected by the intensity of the light source and the sensitivity of the instrument. Secondly, the establishment of an internal standard greatly attenuates the interference of variable factors such as probe concentration, temperature, polarity, environmental pH, etc [31]. Therefore, the sensitivity, selectivity and the dynamic range of the ratio fluorescence probe are greatly improved. The design of ratiometric fluorescent probes is of great significance for the quantitative analysis of DA.

Many ratiometric fluorescent probes are based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. The design of such probes needs to find suitable fluorescent materials, and to meet the conditions for the FRET effect to occur [7,28,29]. That is, (1) the fluorescence spectrum of the donor should overlap with the excitation spectrum of the acceptor; (2) the donor and acceptor must be arranged in an appropriate manner, and the two must be close enough. Study found that the reported emission spectra of many carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are usually in the blue region, and the excitation spectrum of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) is just in this range. In addition, CQDs and AuNCs are easily modified as there are many functional groups on the surface of them. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CQDs and AuNCs are favored by researchers and used to the detection of DA [7], hydrogen peroxide [32,33], biomercapto molecules [34], metal ions [35,36], etc. For example, Lu et al. [37] developed a dual-emission nanoparticles comprised of carbon dots and AuNCs using seed growth co-microwave method, with carbon dot as donor and AuNCs as acceptor. Based on FRET effect, it was used to construct mesoporous structured ratiometric fluorescent probe for high sensitive and selective detection of bisphenol A with a detection limit of 29 nM and can achieve visual monitoring. However, their applications still require further development, particularly those based on the FRET effect. Therefore, it is feasible to select CQDs and AuNCs as donors and acceptors to prepare ratiometric fluorescent probes based on the FRET effect to realize the detection of DA.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI) is currently known as the cationic organic polymer with the highest charge density, high adhesion and high chemical reactivity. The molecular structure of PEI contains primary amine, secondary amine, tertiary amine and other groups. It can be well connected to the generated CQDs during pyrolysis with citric acid, and can modify many active functional groups of CQDs [38]. Recently, there are a number of ligands, such as glutathione [39], small thiolate ligands [40,41] and proteins [42], that have been used to synthesize highly luminescent Au NCs. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), as a kind of biomacromolecule, contains 583 amino acid residues and some of them have high reactivity [42]. Thus it can reduce gold ions and connect to AuNCs firmly through S-Au bond. In the synthesis of NCs, BSA was selected as the ligand to prepare AuNCs, which not only prevents AuNCs from agglomerating, but also provides the groups of coupling to CQDs [7].

In summary, amidated modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and bovine serum albumin-coated gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) were prepared, then they were combined via EDC/NHS coupling to obtain purple fluorescence emission CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid. Based on the principle of FRET, CQDs-AuNCs has two emission peaks of 446 nm and 670 nm under excitation of 397 nm. DA can quench the fluorescence of CQDs-AuNCs. According to this phenomenon, CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was applied as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to quantitatively detect DA (Scheme 1 ).

1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxy-sulfosuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, fraction V), polyethyleneimine (PEI), l-dopamine hydrochloride (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), potassium chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phenylalanine (Phe), l-Lysine (Lys), l-Aspartic acid (Asp), l-Proline (Pro), l-Tryptophan (Trp), l-Glutamine (Gln), l-Valine (Val), l-Tyrptophan (Tyr), l-Arginine (Arg), l-Threonine (Thr), l-Isoleucine (Ile), l-Leucine (Leu), l-Alanine (Ala), l-Serine (Ser), and Glycine (Gly) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer (PB, pH 7.2, 0.02 M) comprising NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 was used for the detection experiments. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm (at 25 °C) was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Thermo scientific, USA) and used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence assays were performed on F97pro spectrometer. The size and morphology were characterized by JM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM). A dilute solution of a certain concentration was applied to a 300 mesh copper mesh covered with a carbon film and dried at room temperature. KBr pellet was used for Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements on a Nicolet 5700 instrument. The fluorescent lifetime was determined by FLS1000 (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements were performed on a Brookhaven NanoBrook Omni (Brookhaven Instruments Inc, USA).

CQDs were prepared by pyrolysis using citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as raw materials according to previous report with slight modification [38]. Typically, CA (2.0 g) and PEI (1.0 g) were dissolved evenly in 20 mL hot water at 70 °C in a beaker. Then, the beaker was placed in a heating mantle and heated at 200 °C for about 30 min to evaporate water to form a gel. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of ultrapure water was added to the formed pale-yellow gel, and continued to heat. After repeating the process about 10 times in 3 h, the color of the gel turned orange, indicating the formation of CQDs. The prepared CQDs was diluted with ultrapure water to a 20 mL solution and purified for 24 h by a dialysis membrane having a molecular weight cut off of 3.5 KDa. The obtained CQDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The BSA-mediated AuNCs were prepared according to previous classic report [42]. Briefly, 250 mg of BSA was placed in a 20 mL glass flask and 5 mL of ultrapure water was added. The glass flask was placed in a 40 °C water bath and magnetically stirred until the BSA was completely dissolved. Then, 5 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 solution was added dropwise to mix thoroughly. After 2 min, 0.25 mL of 1 M NaOH solution was added dropwise, and the BSA-encapsulated gold nanoclusters were prepared by vigorously stirring the reaction for 8 h in the water bath. Subsequently, the samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and purified by membrane dialysis (1000 Da). Finally, AuNCs were freeze-dried and dissolved in ultrapure water for future use.

The CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was prepared via a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction. Firstly, 10 mg of each of EDC and NHS was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, respectively. After the solution was completely dissolved, the two solutions were mixed and thoroughly stirred to form coupling agent of EDC/NHS. Briefly, EDC/NHS and AuNCs solution with a volume ratio of 0.25:3 were mixed and stirred well for 5 min to activate the carboxyl group. A certain volume of CQDs (AuNCs: CQDs = 3:0.6 v/v) was slowly added to the above solution to obtain a turbid mixture. 2 M NaOH was added dropwise until the solution became clear and transparent. The solution was stirred under room temperature in the dark for 2 h. The prepared CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was then purified by membrane dialysis (3.5 kDa) and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity test of CQDs-AuNCs to DA
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL freshly prepared CQDs-AuNCs hybrid were diluted to 3 mL with PB (pH 7.2, 0.02 M). Different concentration of DA was added to CQDs-AuNCs solution successively using micro syringe. The resulting solutions were mixed uniformly and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. For selectivity test, fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrids were measured in the presence of DA (0.2 mM) or other interfering substances with a concentration of 1.0 mM. The fluorescence ratiometric values (F446/F670) of mixture solutions were calculated. All fluorescence emission was excitated at 397 nm with a slit width of 5 nm. All tests were performed at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assays of DA detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh serum was centrifuged, diluted 100 times with PB, and then used as a solvent for DA. Because of the variability of the chemical composition of biological samples, standard addition method was used for determination. Different concentrations of DA were added to the serum fixed the concentration as 0.1, 10 and 150 μM and then the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-AuNCs interacting with serum were monitored. According to the plotted linear relationship between CDA and the ratio F446/F670, the value of CDA were calculated.

CQDs samples were prepared by pyrolysis of PEI and CA. As shown in Fig. 1 A inset, CQDs was a pale yellow-green transparent liquid under daylight; it showed strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Fluorescence spectroscopy of CQDs showed that the optimal excitation wavelength of CQDs was 397 nm and the emission wavelength was 471 nm. AuNCs samples were successfully prepared in an alkaline environment with BSA as a protective and reducing agent at 40 °C. As shown in Fig. 1B inset, the prepared sample was a tan transparent liquid under daylight and exhibited a bright red light under 365 nm UV light. The excitation wavelength of AuNCs was 471 nm with the emission wavelength 670 nm (Fig. 1A). Interestingly, the optimal excitation wavelength of AuNCs is the same as the emission wavelength of CQDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and Au NCs was almost unchanged within 10 min of continuous detection (Fig. S1) and after sored in 4 ℃ about one month. The CQDs-AuNCs was prepared by combining AuNCs and CQDs in a certain ratio with the EDC/NHS coupling. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, CQDs-AuNCs exhibited purple fluorescence under UV light. At an excitation wavelength of 397 nm, the nanohybrids had two distinct emission peaks, one at 446 nm, in the blue region attributed to CQDs, and the other at 670 nm, in the red region rooting in AuNCs. Importantly, the dual emission peaks of CQDs-AuNCs are changed negligibly during 10 min of continuous scanning (Fig. 1D). Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-AuNCs was recorded over about 30 days, showing almost no change (Fig. S2). These results indicated the good stability of nanohybrids.

The FRET effect must satisfy the conditions: (1) the emission spectrum of the donor should overlap with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor; (2) the energy donor and the energy acceptor must be close enough (less than 10 nm) [7]. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the CQDs emission spectrum almost coincides with the AuNCs excitation spectrum in 400–500 nm, and the optimal wavelengths of both are 470 nm, which is fully meet the FRET effect generation conditions (1) and indicated that there could be an FRET effect between them. The morphology and size of AuNCs, CQDs and CQDs-AuNCs were characterized by TEM. The size of AuNCs and CQDs are about 1.5 nm and 3 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). The nanohybrids were evenly distributed, with good dispersion and no obvious aggregation (Fig. 2B). Using Image J software to process the data, the interplanar spacing was 0.24 nm and the particle size distribution ranged from 1.4 nm to 3.8 nm with the average particle size 2.64 ± 0.06 nm (Fig. 2C). Further, the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, AuNCs and CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid were performed to confirm the coupling of CQDs and AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 2D, the absorption peak of AuNCs at 3445 cm−1 is the expansion and contraction of the CN bond of the secondary amine, and the peak at 1652 cm−1 is the expansion of the carboxyl group CO bond. This indicates that the surface of AuNCs has amino groups and carboxyl groups. The peak of CQDs at 1703 cm−1 is the stretching of the CO bond of the carboxylic acid, and the peak at 1188 cm−1 is caused by the in-plane bending of the secondary amine NH bond. After CQDs combined with AuNCs, a distinct peak of 1536 cm−1 appears on CQDs-AuNCs, which attributed to the bending vibration of the NH bond of the secondary amide. This successfully demonstrated the coupling reaction between AuNCs and CQDs is completed. These results meet the FRET effect generation conditions (2).

Fig. 3 shows the comparison of emission spectra of AuNCs, CQDs and CQDs-AuNCs at an excitation wavelength of 397 nm. It can be seen that the peak of the CQDs-AuNCs in the blue region is blue-shifted to 446 nm compared with the peak of CQDs, and the fluorescence intensity is significantly lower than that of CQDs. However, the peak fluorescence intensity of CQDs-AuNCs in the red region is significantly higher than that of the individual AuNCs. It can be analyzed that the emission of CQDs at 470 nm was partly absorbed by AuNCs thus resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the blue region for CQDs-AuNCs. The AuNCs was excited by the absorbed light and emitted strong red fluorescence, which causes that the emission fluorescence intensity of the nanohybrid in the red region was much stronger than that of the AuNCs alone. This indicates that the prepared CQDs-AuNCs do have a FRET effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the sensing DA
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 4 A, while 5 mM of DA interacted with the AuNCs solution, the fluorescence emission was hardly changed. This is because AuNCs were coated with BSA and isolated from DA. However, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 460 nm decreased by 50 % after the addition of 5 mM DA. Similarly, after adding 5 mM DA to CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid, the fluorescence intensity at 446 nm was significantly reduced. The reduction at 670 nm is because CQDs-AuNCs have a FRET effect, the decrease of fluorescence emission in the donor of CQDs causes the FRET effect to be weakened so that the fluorescence emission in the acceptor of AuNCs also decrease. Fluorescence responses of AuNCs, CQDs and CQDs-AuNCs on DA could also be visually observed (Fig. 4D). The change of CQDs-AuNCs was more obvious (b and b’) compared with CQDs (a and a’) and AuNCs (c and c’). Based on this phenomenon, we speculate that CQDs-AuNCs has a more sensitive and rapid fluorescence response to DA than a single CQDs. DA-dependent fluorescence intensities and color changes of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid manifested the potential for ratiometric fluorescence detection of DA.

In order to better understand the interaction mechanism of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid with DA, TEM, DLS, time-resolved luminescence decays and zeta potential were performed. TEM characterization results showed that the particle size of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid is about 3.17 ± 0.05 nm after interacting with DA (Fig. S4), indicating that no aggregation occurred. It should be noted that the DLS test showed that the hydrodynamic particle size did not change before and after DA addition, but the hydrodynamic particle size was hundreds of nanometers, maybe due to the biomolecule BSA on the surface of CQDs-AuNCs. These results show that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs-AuNCs is not caused by material aggregation. According to literature reports, DA is easily to be oxidized to dopaquinone by dissolved oxygen, making it a good electron acceptor [13]. Zeta potential test showed that the zeta potential of dopamine is 3.96 ± 0.23 mV, indicates that the dopamine is oxidized. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of CQDs-AuNCs is changed form 10.42 ± 2.90 mV to 20.65 ± 0.4 mV after interacted with DA. These results manifest that electrons are transferred from CQDs-AuNCs to DA [14]. Therefore, with addition of DA, the electronic states of CQDs-AuNCs will change, thus resulting in the fluorescence change. In order to further explore the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-AuNCs before and after interacting with DA were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5 and Table S1, the mean lifetime of CQDs-AuNCs and CQDs-AuNCs + DA were 8.07 ± 0.15 and 8.06 ± 0.16 ns, respectively. As the static quenching mechanism usually keep the fluorescence lifetime constant or decreases [43], the data confirmed that the fluorescence quenching must be involving the static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity test of the DA probe
ce:section.ce:para: Because CQDs has high fluorescence intensity under acidic conditions, while AuNCs has high fluorescence intensity under alkali conditions. In order to improve the performance of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrids to DA, the pH value of CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was optimized. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the response of F446/F670 to DA was pH sensitive. It shows that the fluorescence ratio in neutral condition (pH 7.2) was most obvious compared to acidic and alkaline conditions; after adding 0.3 mM DA, an obvious sensing response also occurred in neutral conditions (pH 7.2). Therefore, pH 7.2 PB was selected for this detection system. It should be noted that when pH is less than 6, CQDs-AuNCs showed obvious turbidity and the fluorescence was almost unchanged with the addition of dopamine.

The CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was further constructed as a novel fluorescence probe for DA detection. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of two emission peaks reduced with the increase of DA concentration. Otherwise, CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid could realize the fluorescence visual imaging (Fig. 5A inset) of DA. The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio value (F446/F670) and DA concentration (CDA) was examined. Fluorescence intensity ratio value vs. CDA was linear over the range of 2.66 nM–0.18 mM with a calibration equation of F446/F670 = −9267.7CDA + 5.31 (R² = 0.99), 0.511–3.79 mM with a calibration equation of F446/F670 = −442.9CDA + 3.34 (R² = 0.97) and 4.87–13.1 mM with a calibration equation of F446/F670 = −58.7CDA + 1.79 (R² = 0.97). The detection limit was measured appropriately to be 2.66 nM [14]. According to the reported paper, the different linear response at three concentration ranges maybe attribute to the change of adsorption rate between DA and CQDs-AuNCs [7]. As summarized in Table 1 , this ratiometric probe is comparable to other relevant fluorescent probes in terms of detection limit and linear range. Moreover, this ratiometric fluorescence sensing system takes less time and has good stability. These results demonstrated the feasibility of the fluorescent probe for DA detection.

To investigate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, the response of other molecules was investigated, such as amino acids (including Phe, Lys, Asp, Pro, Glu, Trp, Gln, Val, Tyr, Ary, Thr, Ile, Leu, Ala, Ser, and Gly), ascorbic acid (AA), and ions (including K+, Na+, and Mg2+). As clearly illustrated in Fig. 6 , 0.2 mM of DA caused an obvious decrease in ratio of fluorescence peak intensities F446/F670. While, 1.0 mM of each interfering substances only caused slight changes in F446/F670. In other words, the detection of DA was not interfered by these competitors, even though they were at 5 times higher concentrations. These results further indicated the ratio fluorescent probes have high selectivity and sensitivity to DA response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe detection of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real human serum samples were diluted about 100 times with PB without further purification. Then, human serum samples spiked DA were dripped into CQDs-AuNCs to obtain a series of sample-probe systems. According to the linear relationship between F446/F670 and DA concentration in Fig. 5B, the concentration of DA in real samples was obtained by detecting fluorescence emission spectra of sample-probe systems. As shown in Table 2 , the results calculated were consistent with the spiked values, the recovery and RSD values were in the ranges of 98–105 % and 2.6–4.3 %, respectively. These results indicate that the matrix effect of serum dilution does not affect the detection of DA. In other words, the presence of potential interference has almost no influence on DA detection.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescent probe CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was constructed through facile coupling of blue-emitting CQDs with red-emitting AuNCs. DA could reduce the fluorescence of CQDs through the electron transfer from CQDs-AuNCs to DA and inhibit the FRET process between CQDs and AuNCs thus leading to fluorescence intensity change. By this performance, CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrid is selective for DA with a detection limit of 2.66 nM over the linear ranges of 2.66 nM–0.18 mM, 0.511–3.79 mM and 4.87–13.1 mM. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence probe has good anti-interference ability. The detection of DA in serum samples verified the effectiveness of the application of the probe.

Yuanyuan Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Hedan Xu: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yuling Yang: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Fengmei Zhu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Yunxun Pu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoshuang You: Methodology, Project administration, Resources. Xiaoling Liao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Key Foundation of China (11532004), Natural Science Foundation General Project of CQ in China (CSTC2019JCYJ-msxmX0221, CSTC 2018JCYJAX0286), Chongqing Technology Innovation and Application Development Project (CSTC 2019JSCX-msxmX0231) and Postgraduate Science and Technology Innovation Program of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (YKJCX1920205).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113195.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
